Pepper__bell___Bacterial_spot.name: Bacterial Spot
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves
Spots turn dark brown/black with light centers
Leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely
Scabby, dark lesions on the pepper fruit
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides immediately
Remove and destroy severely infected plant debris
Do not compost infected plants
prevention: Plant pathogen-free seeds and resistant varieties
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation
Avoid overhead watering to keep foliage dry

Pepper__bell___healthy.name: Healthy Plant
symptoms: Vibrant green leaves
Strong, upright stems
No visible spots, wilting, or pest damage
treatment: No active treatment required.
prevention: Maintain consistent soil moisture
Apply balanced fertilizers as needed
Monitor weekly for early signs of pests

Potato___Early_blight.name: Early Blight
symptoms: Small, dark, brown/black spots on older leaves
Spots enlarge to form concentric rings (target board pattern)
Lower leaves yellow, dry up, and die
treatment: Apply fungicides like Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil early
Remove and safely destroy severely infected lower leaves
prevention: Practice 2-3 year crop rotation
Ensure proper spacing for air circulation
Avoid overhead watering and water at the base of the plant

Potato___Late_blight.name: Late Blight
symptoms: Irregular, water-soaked spots on leaves
White fuzzy fungal growth on the underside of leaves
Dark brown, shallow, and firm lesions on tubers
treatment: Immediately destroy infected plants (do not compost)
Apply preventative fungicides containing chlorothalonil or copper
Harvest tubers only after vines are completely dead
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes
Destroy volunteer potatoes and cull piles
Avoid planting tomatoes near potatoes

Potato___healthy.name: Healthy Plant
symptoms: Vibrant green foliage
Vigorous growth
No signs of rot, spotting, or insect damage
treatment: No active treatment required.
prevention: Ensure proper hilling around the base
Maintain regular watering schedule
Monitor for potato beetles and aphids

Tomato_Bacterial_spot.name: Bacterial Spot
symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves
Spots may have a yellow halo and cause leaves to drop
Small, raised, scab-like spots on green fruit
treatment: Spray with copper fungicides mixed with Mancozeb
Prune affected leaves immediately
Sanitize pruning tools between cuts
prevention: Use drip irrigation instead of overhead sprinklers
Plant resistant tomato varieties
Clear garden debris at the end of the season

Tomato_Early_blight.name: Early Blight
symptoms: Bullseye patterned spots on lower/older leaves
Yellowing of tissue surrounding the spots
Dark, sunken lesions on stems at the soil line
treatment: Apply fungicides like copper, chlorothalonil, or Bacillus subtilis
Prune off the lowest branches to prevent soil contact
Apply organic mulch around the base of the plant
prevention: Space plants properly to allow sunlight and airflow
Practice 3-year crop rotation
Stake or cage tomatoes to keep foliage off the ground

Tomato_Late_blight.name: Late Blight
symptoms: Large, pale green to brown spots on leaves
White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides in high humidity
Firm, dark brown spots on the tomato fruit
treatment: Immediate application of copper-based fungicides
Remove and bag infected plants immediately to stop spread
Do not save seeds from infected plants
prevention: Plant late-blight resistant varieties
Ensure excellent airflow and reduce humidity
Keep foliage dry

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.name: Leaf Mold
symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on the upper leaf surface
Velvety, olive-green to brown mold on the underside of leaves
Infected leaves eventually wither and die
treatment: Improve ventilation and air circulation immediately
Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil if severe
Remove heavily infected leaves
prevention: Maintain low humidity, especially in greenhouses
Prune tomato plants to open up the canopy
Water at the base early in the day

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.name: Septoria Leaf Spot
symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark borders and gray/tan centers
Tiny black specks (fruiting bodies) in the center of spots
Starts on lower leaves and moves up the plant
treatment: Apply fungicides (copper or chlorothalonil) every 7-10 days
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Wash hands and tools after handling infected plants
prevention: Apply heavy mulch to prevent soil splashing onto leaves
Water at the soil level
Practice crop rotation

Tomato_Spider_mites_Two_spotted_spider_m.name: Two-Spotted Spider Mites
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white speckles (stippling) on leaves
Fine, silky webbing on the undersides of leaves or stems
Leaves turn bronze, yellow, and dry up
treatment: Spray foliage thoroughly with insecticidal soap or neem oil
Use a strong jet of water to wash mites off the plant
Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) in greenhouses
prevention: Keep plants well-watered (mites thrive in hot, dry, dusty conditions)
Wash dust off foliage periodically
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators

Tomato__Target_Spot.name: Target Spot
symptoms: Small brown spots with concentric rings on leaves
Spots may merge, causing large dead areas
Dark, sunken lesions on the fruit with distinct rings
treatment: Apply broad-spectrum fungicides (chlorothalonil or mancozeb)
Prune plants to improve airflow and drying
Remove infected leaves as soon as they appear
prevention: Provide adequate spacing between plants
Avoid prolonged periods of leaf wetness
Destroy all crop residue at the end of the season

Tomato__Tomato_YellowLeaf__Curl_Virus.name: Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
symptoms: Severe stunting of the plant
Leaves curl upward and become cup-shaped
Leaves turn pale yellow with green veins
Flowers drop, severely reducing fruit yield
treatment: There is no cure for this virus.
Immediately pull up and bag/destroy infected plants to protect others
Spray for whiteflies (the vector) using insecticidal soaps
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties
Control whitefly populations using yellow sticky traps
Use reflective mulches to deter insects

Tomato__Tomato_mosaic_virus.name: Mosaic Virus
symptoms: Light and dark green mottled areas on leaves (mosaic pattern)
Leaves may look fern-like, distorted, or puckered
Stunted growth and reduced fruit yield
Internal browning of the fruit
treatment: There is no cure for this virus.
Carefully remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Do not smoke near plants (virus is transmitted via tobacco products)
prevention: Wash hands thoroughly with soap before handling plants
Disinfect pruning tools with a bleach solution
Use TMV-resistant seed varieties

Tomato_healthy.name: Healthy Plant
symptoms: Deep green, lush leaves
Strong, sturdy stems
Vibrant flowering and fruiting without spots or decay
treatment: No active treatment required.
prevention: Deep, consistent watering at the base
Regular application of tomato-specific fertilizer
Proper staking or trellising for support
